intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to Swift elevates code from a web environment to a highly compiled, type-safe system language tailored for Apple platforms and modern cloud architectures. Developers execute this migration to build high-performance, native applications with zero runtime engine overhead. The change introduces developers to strict nominal types, explicit value immutability patterns, and advanced compiler guarantees.

diffs.from: Static structural checking with structural duck-typing rules, completely stripped prior to runtime.
diffs.to: Strict nominal static type safety featuring generic protocol extensions, associated types, and strict type safety controls.

diffs.from: C-style syntax using explicit variable markers, class structures, and arrow declarations.
diffs.to: Modern, streamlined syntax with parameter labels, explicit switch cases, and trailing closure configurations.

diffs.from: Automated runtime generational garbage collection managing an application heap dynamically.
diffs.to: Deterministic Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) tracking and deallocating object instances at runtime without stop-the-world sweeps.

diffs.from: Single-thread runtime event loop executing micro-task scheduling queues asynchronously.
diffs.to: Structured concurrency framework natively utilizing async/await keyword pairs, Task queues, and state-isolating Actors.

diffs.from: EcmaScript core types heavily supplemented via external open-source module ecosystems.
diffs.to: Comprehensive core toolkit covering cryptographic functions, string unicode engines, and highly optimized collection structures.

examples.title: Struct Definitions and Optional States
examples.description: Modeling data entities with explicit optional type tracking safely.

examples.title: Safe Pattern Guard Controls
examples.description: Unwrapping conditional states early to prevent cascading code indentation.

tips.title: Understand Structs and Classes
tips.body: Swift prefers value types over reference types. Declare data models as `struct` rather than `class` to gain automatic thread safety and avoid reference-counting overhead; structs are copied on assignment.

tips.title: Master Argument Parameter Labels
tips.body: Swift functions use external argument labels for parameters (e.g., `func greet(to name: String)`). When invoking the method, you must explicitly include the label tag name in the argument parameters: `greet(to: "Alice")`.

tips.title: Prevent Retain Cycles in Closures
tips.body: Because Swift tracks objects via ARC reference counters, passing long-lived classes into closures can cause cyclical leaks. Prevent this by defining explicit capture lists like `[weak self]` at the start of the block.

tips.title: Use guard for Early Return Paths
tips.body: Replace traditional nested `if` statements with Swift's `guard` statement. This structure forces an early return block, keeping your primary code paths flat, clean, and highly readable.
